lastUpdateSummary.headline: Hormuz access is increasingly permissioned, with Iranian-approved and shadow-fleet movements still getting through while most compliant traffic stays choked off.
lastUpdateSummary.body: A newly reviewed Maritime Executive analysis adds vessel-level detail to the dashboard picture. It says two Indian-flagged LPG carriers were escorted through after Iranian approval, identifies the Saudi-laden VLCC Smyrni and sanctioned product tankers Ocean Guardian and Blooming Dale among the ships that appear to have transited, and notes Lloyd’s List Intelligence has detected nine dark-fleet transits in March while assessing about 45 in total. For the dashboard, the main takeaway is that low observed counts should be read as a selective-access regime shaped by Iranian permission, AIS suppression and shadow-fleet behaviour, not as a simple binary closure.

last24hSummary.headline: The last 24 hours point to a more selective and opaque passage regime alongside worsening storage stress across Gulf exporters.
last24hSummary.body: Recent reporting already showed zero AIS-confirmed crossings on 14 March, continued Kharg activity and fast-rising upstream storage pressure. The additional high-signal detail now is that some voyages still appear to be clearing Hormuz under Iranian approval or shadow-fleet conditions, including sanctioned tankers and India-linked LPG movements. That combination matters for interpretation: visible traffic can stay near zero while a narrower set of politically tolerated or lower-visibility voyages continues to move.
